content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : File                                           Date : 1-30-78

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Barbara Campbell /PC

Subject: Reporting Interests in Trusts.

     At its January 26, 1978, meeting, the Fair Political Prac-
tices Commission authorized the staff to draft a regulation describ-
ing when an interest in a trust should be disclosed. Until that
regulation is adopted, the Commission has allowed the staff to give
the following advice.

     Any filer who has a present interest in a trust, because
he or she receives income or principal, must disclose the interest
in the trust.

     If a filer is a beneficiary of a trust, the filer will
have to report if he or she receives income currently from the trust
or if he or she has an irrevocable right to receive income or
principal at a later time. An individual has an irrevocable right
to receive income or principal if no one else can change that
provision in the trust agreement. However, if an individual is named
as a future beneficiary under a revocable trust and does not current-
ly receive any income or principal, that individual will not have to
disclose his or her interest in the trust.

     If the filer is the creator of a trust, the filer will have
to disclose an interest in the trust if the trust is revocable if
the filer has the right to receive income or principal; if the filer
has any reversionary or remainder interests; or if the filer has
a power of appointment over the trust agreement. Therefore if the
filer creates an irrevocable trust in which he or she retains no
powers and from which he or she receives no income or principal,
the filer will not have to disclose any interest in that irrevocable
trust.

     If the filer only has a life estate to receive income
from the trust, the filer will only report the income he or she re-
ceives from the trust. If the filer has a remainder interest, the
filer will report his or her pro rata share of the investments and
interests in real property held by the trust.

Memorandum to File
1-30-78
p.2

The standards are shown in the following example: A
creates an irrevocable trust in which B has a life estate and C and
D are named as remainder beneficiaries. If A, B, C and D are filers
of Statement of Economic Interests, B, C and D will have to report
an interest in the trust. B will have to report an interest in
the trust because B receives a life estate. C and D have an abso-
lute right to the remainder interest. B will report the income
or principal which he or she receives from the trust. C and D will
report the value of their remainder interest in the trust. The
remainder interest can be reported by C and D in any one of the
following ways: a) they could each report 50% of the value of the
principal of the trust, or b) they could actually determine their
interest in the trust by subtracting the actuarial value of B's
life estate interest from the value of the trust.

If A had established an irrevocable trust in which B had
not only a life estate but also had a power of appointment, that
is the right to change the remainderpersons, then C and D, who are
named as remainderpersons under the terms of the trust, will not
have to report their interest in the trust. Their right to the re-
mainder interest is not irrevocable. B can change the names of the
remainderpersons.

In the above examples, the interests in the trusts would
be reported because the filers had 10% or greater interest in the
trust. The specifics of reporting income, interests in real property
and/or investments of a trust are described in the attached
November 25, 1977, memorandum.

These are the general guidelines approved for reporting
interests in trusts. The Commission will be adopting a regulation
clarifying the reporting. In the meantime, specific questions should
be directed to the Legal Division.


BC:jo
Attachment
cc Ted Prim [/]
    Gina Todd
    Alice Hughes

At its January 26, 1978, meeting, the Fair Political Practices Commission authorized the staff to draft a regulation describing when an interest in a trust should be disclosed. Until that regulation is adopted, the Commission has allowed the staff to give the following advice. Any filer who has a present interest in a trust, because he or she receives income or principal, must disclose the interest in the trust. If a filer is a beneficiary of a trust, the filer will have to report if he or she receives income currently from the trust or if he or she has an irrevocable right to receive income or the principal at a later time. An individual has an irrevocable right to receive income or the principal if no one else can change that provision in the trust agreement. However, if an individual is named as a future beneficiary under a revocable trust and does not currently receive any income or principal, that individual will not have to disclose his or her interest in the trust. If the filer is the creator of a trust, the filer will have to disclose an interest in the trust if the trust is revocable; if the filer has the right to receive income or principal; if the filer has any reversionary or remainder interests; or if the filer has a power of appointment over the trust agreement. Therefore if the filer creates an irrevocable trust in which he or she retains no powers and from which he or she receives no income or principal, the filer will not have to disclose any interest in that irrevocable trust. If the filer only has a life estate to receive income from the trust, the filer will only report the income he or she receives from the trust. If the filer has a remainder interest, the filer will report his or her pro rata share of the investments and interests in real property held by the trust. The standards are shown in the following example: A creates an irrevocable trust in which B has a life estate and C and D are named as remainder beneficiaries. If A, B, C and D are filers of Statements of Economic Interests, B, C and D will have to report an interest in the trust. B will have to report an interest in the trust because B receives a life estate. C and D have an absolute right to the remainder interest. B will report the income or principal which he or she receives from the trust. C and D will report the value of their remainder interest in the trust. The remainder interest can be reported by C and D in any one of the following ways: a) they could each report 50% of the value of the principal of the trust, or b) they could actually determine their interest in the trust
